pattern: Anchoring each subarray at its left end and sweeping the right end forward while maintaining a live frequency table lets you track, incrementally, both how many distinct values are currently in view and how many of them still haven't reached the minimum-occurrence bar -- so a window only counts once both of those tallies land exactly where they need to.
hints: For each left boundary, extend the right boundary one step at a time, updating a frequency map instead of recomputing it from scratch.
Keep two running tallies alongside the frequency map: the number of distinct values currently present, and how many of those currently have fewer than the required minimum occurrences.

A window counts only when the distinct tally equals the target exactly and the under-threshold tally has dropped to zero -- and once the distinct tally exceeds the target for a given left boundary, every further extension will too, so you can stop extending early.

edge_inputs.description: two subarrays qualify with exactly two distinct values each appearing twice
m relaxed to 1, reduces to counting subarrays with exactly k distinct values
k exceeds the total number of distinct values present -- no window can ever qualify
every value distinct so no value can ever reach m=2 occurrences
all identical values -- only k=1 windows can ever qualify, and only once m is satisfied
k equal to array length forces the only candidate window to be the whole array
m at the array-length ceiling, only the full array (if uniform) could ever qualify

stress.description: moderate-size arrays over a small value alphabet so k and m are frequently satisfiable, keeping the differential brute-force oracle tractable
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 160))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 6) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 4)
    m = rng.randint(1, 4)
    return [nums, k, m]

stress.description: wider value range with tighter m to stress the deficient-count bookkeeping
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 140))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    m = rng.randint(1, 3)
    return [nums, k, m]
